[h3]TL;DR :[/h3] Core features and heists are top tier and the game is definitely worth picking already, but the game still have a long road before reaching the level of payday 2 in term of content. With more content, it will definitely become a worthy successor of Payday 2.  ______________________________  [b]The core gameplay features are solid and represent a major improvement over Payday 2.[/b] Stealth has received a lot of attention with new mechanics that make it more deep, tactical and immersive, while loud gameplay feels much more like an actual heist. One feature I particularly enjoy is the hostage system. When things go loud, first responders arrive before the full police assault begins, and you can trade hostages to delay the assault and buy your crew more time. It adds a layer of strategy and reinforces the heist fantasy.  [b]The heists themselves are excellent and easily stand alongside the ones from Payday 2.[/b] From what i've seen so far, they feature multiple objectives and approaches that encourage replayability.  [b]That said, the game still needs way more content, as it feels quite bare-bones compared to Payday 2.[/b] Features such as AI team mates loadout, heists pre-planning like in P2, and the jukebox feature would be welcome additions. We need a larger selection of masks, outfits, weapons, attachments, gadgets, heists, and heisters as well. (Both heisters from Payday 2 and new heisters please)  I've picked the game + dlcs very cheaply during Steam sales and considering the amount of content currently available, it's well worth the asking price but there is still a long road for it to reach the level of Payday 2 in term of content

I'd also say it doesn't run that well, I've experienced stuttering and heavy lag spikes from time to time (sometimes the screen froze for a whole second), not enough that it has a big impact personally, but still there and very noticeable.  Despite its flaws, I still enjoy playing the game, I'm having fun, so that's why I'm writing a good review. If they fix some bugs and add (a lot) more content then this could definitely be as good of a game as Payday 2 was.

PAYDAY 3 is a cooperative first-person heist shooter developed by Starbreeze Studios and published by Deep Silver. It continues the franchise’s focus on coordinated crime operations, stealth or loud approaches, and high-risk, high-reward bank robberies.  The core gameplay loop revolves around planning and executing heists with a team of four players. Each mission supports multiple approaches, allowing squads to go in quietly with stealth tactics or escalate into full firefights when things go wrong. When coordination is strong, the game delivers tense and satisfying cooperative moments, especially during escalation phases where objectives stack and pressure increases rapidly.  Gunplay is responsive and functional, with a focus on controlled firefights rather than arcade chaos. Progression systems and loadout customization allow players to tailor builds around stealth, combat, or hybrid roles. However, compared to its predecessor, some players feel that launch content was limited, with fewer heists and less variety in mission structure.  Visually, PAYDAY 3 is a step up from PAYDAY 2, with improved lighting, environments, and character models. On capable hardware, it can run at high frame rates at max settings, delivering smooth performance during most missions, though server dependency and online stability have historically impacted the overall experience.  The biggest criticisms center around content depth and long-term engagement. At launch, the game felt underfilled compared to expectations, and progression systems were viewed by some players as restrictive or grind-heavy. Post-launch updates have aimed to address these concerns, but opinions remain divided depending on current content state.  Score: 7/10 – A solid co-op heist shooter with strong core mechanics and tense gameplay, but held back by limited content variety and a rougher overall launch structure.

Most overhated game of this generation.     When this game released it was in a very bare bones, confusingly designed state, and while the criticisms that were thrown against it at that time were very valid, I've personally always thought this game had a lot of potential and it's finally starting to live up to that potential.     This game is very different to Payday 2, and this is something you have to accept if you want to enjoy it. The vision of Payday 3 is a lot closer to the original Payday 1, Payday 3 is a game where resources are scarce and must be used efficiently in order to make it out alive and filthy rich. The heists are long and drawn out and difficult, with each passing moment your chances of making it out get slimmer as your resources continue to dwindle, but the longer you stay, the higher the potential reward you'll get if you do make it out alive. Compare this to Payday 2, where resources are abundant, damage numbers and health pools are absurdly high and the meta is a lot more focused on abusing busted perk decks rather than managing resources. Payday 3 is very different to 2, and both games are fun in their own way and offer something different. The first step to enjoying PD3 is understanding that it's different and that's not a bad thing.     When Payday 3 works, I think it's an insanely fun, addictive experience. When PD3 works I think it is peak Payday, I think it is the original vision of Payday The Heist brought back to modernity with stunningly gorgeous weapon animations and feel, and the possibility of really interesting, varied and strategic builds with the new reworked and vastly superior skill tree system.     The biggest criticism I can throw at this game right now is that it is still light on content, and it still has a bunch of confusing design decisions that are 'left over' from it's launch state. I do think it's unfair to compare this game to current Payday 2, when you take into consideration that PD2 has been in active development for like 2 decades and that game was also dramatically different when it was first released.     This game was hated on release, for valid reasons, but, I've felt back then and still feel now that this game is just brimming with potential, and it is more than deserving of a redemption arc. Try to forget about what this game was like in its launch state, give it an honest shot and you'll see that it's in a dramatically better state than when it was released, and in my opinion, this game is peak Payday.

I was never exposed to PAYDAY 3's launch, so my opinion on the game is purely as a new player (played a lot of PD2, including the super secret). A lot of the mechanics and heists have been reworked since the launch so most of the old reviews don't really hold up to the current state of the game. I do miss perk decks though and hope that Starbreeze finds a way to implement a unique system similar to it, overkill weapons are fun but there's only 5.5 of them in the game (the Interceptor doesn't really count). Sociopath is really really awesome as an overkill weapon and I hope we get more cool ideas like it in DLCs.  I would recommend buying the game on sale.

The game is snappy, fun, and easy to get the hang of. They've improved every aspect of the game from launch and continue to do so! From the UI now being very clear and concise, (something the prior game struggled with) to the skills system moving to a more similar system to the second game. I absolutely adored PAYDAY2, and coming over to PAYDAY3, there are noticeable improvements. The movement feels fluid, gunplay feels satisfying and rewarding, and stealth offers some interesting new mechanics (like lockpicking & glass cutting minigames - far better than watching a circle fill in PAYDAY2 lol), and the graphics and level design... oh my, the visuals... well done.   The foundation has been constructed. Dealing with a botched launch and years of deserved criticism, the game feels like it's in a good spot to starting adding some serious content (more weapons & heists pls). You have such potential here, Starbreeze!   Pros: Great FPS gameplay Rewarding mechanics Beautiful graphics New takes on previous mechanics Interesting level design  Cons: Grouping up with friends can be inconsistent, and the game is prone to disconnects in the lobby Solo mode is still in beta Repetitiveness/Tedious contracts Content (to be contondered!)  Can't wait to see what the future holds :)

Base game has 12 maps with 7 paid DLC maps. 22 free weapons and 16 paid (includes the 2 overkill weapons which is the baseball bat from the Hotline Miami dlc and Swedish Weapon Pack.) All Payday 3 premium dlc has to be bought if you arent playing with a person thats on your steam friends list that owns the dlc. Payday 2 you can literally join any server and play maps you do AND dont own without it forcing you to buy them which has always been a thing in 2.  Nit picks- Mask look odd and dont have great customization, Gunplay is ok but the reload and movement makes the game feel clunky even with perks boosting guns performance because the reloads dont keep up with the flow of the game like Payday 2 even with the perks on to boost reload speed.  TLDR: Payday 2 is all around better with the perk decks and skill tree, tons of maps, tons of weapons, tons of characters, distinct enemies you can easily identify, masks that embarrasses 3's mask even the default masks side by side looks. Better graphics, more things to do in missions than Payday 2. The game itself is good, but it could use more weapons, more missions, and a Steam marketplace. The weapon editing section no longer displays how quiet your weapon is, and there's no detection risk, making the game easier. Of course, it's better to choose the lightest vest, which doesn't slow you down as much as the heaviest. Bodyguards have been buffed; they detect faster and have better hearing (when sprinting, they can hear it and report it). Bodybags are no longer used, and you carry every civilian and bodyguard you kill on your back. Cameras detect any attempt to open doors with a lockpick (if you don't have the skill tree). You can now also hack cameras, preventing them from detecting you. The game is almost three years old and still has bugs, but they don't impact gameplay. The game itself is great.

When the game first came out, it kind of deserved the hate it got. It had virtually no content, compared to Payday 2. It didn't really offer anything new, except an updated engine.   Came back to it months later, and it's been a blast, if i'm honest. Though, i'm playing Solo because Quickplay never finds anyone. because there's only 800 people playing the game.   I think Payday 3 needs to drop a few free-weekends, and keep doing some sales. If people give it a chance, they'll see there's plenty here to enjoy.   My main gripe is that there's not much to spend your money on. You're hard-locked to a few weapons in the beginning, and you're stuck waiting until others open up, based on level. Also, the weapon attachments are severely lacking compared to other games. it feels about 10 years behind in that regard.   The theorycrafting for your character skills is pretty fun. I've been making a purely stealth/thief build, useful for Solo heists. But i can see lots of other options available.   As Payday 2 was feeling dated to play, 3 seems to have polished things up quite a bit.  The devs just need to quit being silly.  Payday 3 needs to tone down the paid expansions. Release some stuff for free. Maybe do some community events that draw new players in that don't require any money. again, do some free weekends, do some sales. get the payday 2 player-base over here (give them a big discount on 3), get your reviews up to Positive. then maybe you can think of releasing paid dlcs.   There's a good game here. They just need to not kill it with bad corporate decisions, and listen to the player-base.

Here's the thing, yes, Payday 2 will always have a special place in my heart and ultimately be the more polished version of this fanchise... BUT, this game has its own charm in a very "Payday-esque" way. Payday 3 has very similar feels to Hitman which I think is a great change from Payday 2 and offers so much more possibilities when replaying heists and as someone who likes to stealth most jobs, Payday 3 is the much more enjoyable version to accomplish that with. The skill tree and weapon skin/ mask customization are my biggest issues with the game and I really wish they would add post heist loot cards but improvements are yet to come as this game is constantly being updated. Their recent update has allowed for Borderlands 2 type loading times when playing solo and feels insanely fast and runs very smooth even on my ♥♥♥♥ pc. Payday 3 has all the hud/ icon upgrades you would've wanted from Payday 2 with better graphics but just a little less customization and optimization. Games are terrible now a days and I find myself still coming back to this franchise after all these years and I wish more people would give this game a chance and will never fully understand why people hate on this game so much. Starbreeze is a one of a kind Dev Studio and their "no ♥♥♥♥♥ given" attitude towards their market will always have my support.

[h1]Stop looking at the launch reviews, it’s actually good now [/h1]  I get it. I was annoyed at launch too. The servers were busted, the progression system felt like a total grind, and it honestly felt like a unfinished mess. I uninstalled it and almost never looked back.  But I jumped back in a few weeks ago and it’s night and day compared to what it was at release.  First off, they finally fixed the progression. You actually get rewarded for playing the game and finishing heists now instead of doing weird, specific challenges just to level up. It feels like a real Payday game again. The skill system is way better too, so you can actually mess around with different builds that feel useful.  The gunplay was always the best part, but now that the game around it actually works, it’s a blast. The new heists and the changes to the older maps have made it way more fun to replay, too.  Look, if you already own it and you're holding a grudge because of how it launched, just give it one more shot. It’s not perfect, but the devs have clearly been working hard to turn it around and the game is actually in a good spot right now. Don’t miss out just because of a bad first impression. To clarify my stance for everyone asking, I’m a huge Payday 2 fan first and I’m going to keep playing it no matter what happens with this game. I’m not saying Payday 3 is the best thing out there, just that it’s finally in a spot where it’s actually playable and fun.  It still has a massive mountain to climb though and it’s leagues behind 2. The reward system in the second game with the cards felt way more satisfying than what we have here. Also, the live service stuff is still a major frustration. Getting kicked all the way back to the main menu just because you failed to join a lobby is a joke. Not being able to switch your lobby from private to public mid-session or even having basic weapon toggles for lasers and flashlights makes it feel unfinished in a lot of ways.  As for the player count, it’s actually busier than people realize. Steam numbers don't tell the whole story since the console traffic is way higher than the PC side right now.  Bottom line is the game is improving, but I’m still a Payday 2 veteran at heart.

Since release i've said "In a universe that has Payday 2, nobody needs Payday 3." That has changed. Sure, we need more heists and everything, that will always be the case, but the game finally feels like a worthy sequel of my favorite game. Bots seem to always wanna run off the moment you attempt to toss a bag onto their backs, but wishing for less braindead mates is normal. With bullets flying around you, moving multiple hostages at once reasonably feels like herding cats. Take a human shield and cops will rush you. Cloakers finally feel like a threat, how quickly mates get them off actually matters. New enemy units feel good. Don't underestimate the poison gas of naders. Its armor negating damage and movement debuff make reviving mates, downed in there, a costly challenge. Better sweep the surrounding area and go in once it's passed. Techies dispatch drones that stun and damage, but they flood in like all units, no real swarming going on. I generally wished teamtactics among cops felt more like a thing, they do have coms. Have one front pin me down and hold my focus, while others rush their way through my blindspots, make those transition windows smaller. I want light armor cops to get bursts of cloaker speed without their takedown potential.  Long story short, we got our beloved skill trees back, new and improved. Payday feels like Payday again. I'll always be back for more.

I have around 1000 hours in Payday 2. Many bugs from Payday 3 launch have been fixed, the armor system has been reworked, skills have been reworked, Gold & Sharke WiFi hacking has been reworked (thankfully), lockpicking has been updated to be more interesting, p2p matchmaking is about to be implemented. The music by Gustavo is fantastic, Pearl is a great addition to the roster with her stealth dialogues, gunplay is pretty good like in PD2, and compared to PD2, there are a lot of QoL features present, both in stealth and in loud gameplay - doors can be closed, surrendered cops can be moved, weapon reload has states now, consumables can be carried around.  The main downside is the number of heists! MAKE MORE HEISTS - FREE, PAID, DOESN'T MATTER! Without more heists there just isn't enough replayability. Speaking of paid - weapon packs (and possibly weapon mods, maybe even a mod courier DLC which would give more stuff to do on current heists). Weapon customization in general is still clunky, with stats not telling you anything specific and the attachment menu being a pain to navigate.  In short, the game is now decent and you should definitely give it a try if you liked PD2. But please Starbreeze, give PD3 actual funding.

New York styled Pizza:  Pizza dough:  14 g instant yeast  617g water - 95F  950g all-purpose flour  19g fine sea salt  25g granulated sugar  American Pizza Sauce and Toppings:  1 tablespoon (14g) butter  1 tablespoon (9g) extra virgin olive oil  6 cloves garlic, rough chopped  2 teaspoons (4g) red pepper flakes  28 oz (794g) can of crushed tomato  2 teaspoons (2g) dried oregano  1.5 tablespoons (21g) granulated sugar  salt and pepper to taste  Splash of olive oil  2 cups (227g) low moisture mozzarella, fresh grated  2 cups (200g) Monterey jack cheese, fresh grated  1/2 cup (26g) parmigiano reggiano, fresh grated  American Pizza Dough:  Preheat the oven and a baking steel or pizza stone at 500F for 45 minutes before utilizing it.  Whisk yeast and sugar into your water, and allow it to sit for 5 minutes. In a separate bowl, add flour and salt and mix by hand to combine. Pour your water mixture into your flour and mix by hand until you get a rough dough, then transfer to a work surface and knead until it starts to pull away from the work surface and is relatively smooth.  Place your dough in a greased bowl, then cover with plastic wrap and let rise for 1 - 2 hours at room temperature or until double in size.  Punch down your dough and dive into 300g pieces. Form those pieces into balls and place them in a floured proofing pizza box, cover with a lid, and proof at room temperature for 3 - 4 hours. (If you do not have a proofing pizza box, you can use a floured baking sheet, cover it with an inverted baking sheet and wrap it in plastic wrap.)  American Pizza Sauce and Toppings:  In a medium sauce pot, add butter and olive oil. Set that over medium-high heat; once the butter melts add garlic, cook until fragrant, add pepper flakes, and cook, stirring occasionally, until the garlic begins to brown on the edges.  Add crushed tomatoes, oregano, salt, and a splotch of olive oil, boil over medium-high, reduce heat to low, and simmer for 12 minutes, stirring occasionally or until thickened and viscous—season with salt to taste and let it cool down.  Assembly.- Place one of your pizza doughs onto a floured pizza peel, and flour your dough generously.  Punch your dough down in the center, working your way out to create a ¼” wide. Pick up your dough, place it over your closed fists, and rotate it around, gently stretching to 13” to 14”.  Place a couple of spoons of your sauce, starting in the center, spreading in little circles, and working your way to the edge until it gets evenly spread out.  In a medium-size mixing bowl, place mozzarella cheese, Monterey Jack, and Parmigiano; mix until combined. Sprinkle your cheese mixture to your desire and place in the oven onto your baking steel and bake for 5 - 7 minutes or until the edge gets golden brown and the cheese melts.  Enjoy.

I knew Payday 3 was going to have a rough launch, so I stayed away from it. Now that I’ve finally picked it up during a 70% sale, it’s well worth that price, and I’m having a blast. There are a few things I miss from Payday 2—namely some gun customisation options and akimbo weapons—but the new Skills 2.0 system makes for some really interesting gameplay. ------------------------------------------------------------- Pros: Stealth mechanics feel far more advanced, with a lot of QoL improvements that make stealthing feel refreshing Gunplay feels really good QoL additions like damage numbers Somewhat decent build variety ------------------------------------------------------------- Cons: Horrible UI—honestly, how do you downgrade the interface from Payday 2? Gun levelling takes forever Too much content is locked behind DLC; some of what we have now should be part of the base game Piggybacking off the DLC issue: why is Jacket paid DLC again when it was already DLC in Payday 2? ------------------------------------------------------------- Things I’d like to see improved: Gun attachments overhaul, with more variety and better stat balancing UI overhaul—the main menu is confusing and feels like something from an indie game More free content added to the base game; it currently feels a bit barebones A shooting range to test weapons and properly see damage output

Though I didn't buy this game at launch, I heard it was very rough. As a person who hasn't played Payday 2 or 3 that much, here's what I have to say.  I really like the gameplay of Payday 3, and I feel it's a lot better than Payday 2, though the content is much behind Payday 2, and the game still feels very unfinished, which isn't really acceptable for a AAA game. Due to this, this game is not worth the $40. Get it when it is on sale, or play Payday 2 if you want to have a better bang for buck experience. I have hope for this game, and I do have fun with all of the heists I have played so far. My favourite one is Cook Off so far, which is a loud mission where you cook drugs and sell them. I have also played the Payday 2 version of Cook Off, but I still prefer Payday 3's. I wish this game had a lot more content, and it was a disappointment that the game was horrible at launch, but I'm glad the Payday 3 team is still trying, regardless of the low player count. Keep it going, dev's, I hope to see this game succeed.  Try it out for yourself if you are interested, especially since the game is on sale right now. My main recommendation is to only keep it if you actually enjoy the gameplay, and if not, make sure to refund it before you hit two hours to ensure you get your money back. Personally, I like the gameplay of Payday 3 better and find myself having more fun than in Payday 2, though I haven't finished all the heists in Payday 3 yet.

I never look at reviews to make up my mind about any game, so I'll just post my own. I bought this game on day one and from that point on it has basically only improved (the skill tree rework is polarizing, but personally I don't mind it). Initially it was subpar on many fronts, but gradually got to an ok state, at least from a technical standpoint. I can't report any significant bug or malfunction of the game, and I am playing on Linux. The performance is excellent, that's a plus. The graphics looks nice, I like the style, the palette, the lighting, and the sound engineering is surprisingly good.   The only thing is a very small hiccup that happens occasionally and lasts a second, but in a gameplay that's over 30 minutes it doesn't bother me at all. They've added a few maps through DLCs, which I found to be quite expensive, but they are decent content. I like playing stealth and it has a good experience. Overall I think the game is worth it. If you're a Payday 2 veteran, I can see where you come from if you disagree, but regardless, I think it's good fun.

I've read all the comments from release and honestly the game is in a much better state, it provides an experience that's way closer to what Payday 2 SHOULD'VE felt like. Stealth is a lot more fun, you can stealth most maps while unmasked which I feel is truer to the heisting experience. Guard AI is way more enjoyable, hacking cameras is better, lockpicking isn't just a timer you have to wait out, skills are a lot less OP, you can't get a ridiculous amount of dodge chance from just a couple skills.  The bad: Performance can be a bit choppy at times Stealth is genuinely way too fun compared to loud, which wasn't the case in PD2, where loud was just more enjoyable The lowkey expensive DLCs that only offer one heist each and some other options for a seemingly unfinished game is disappointing The amount of heists is a little limited considering this game has been out for 2 years The community is unforgiving and really impatient, in PD2, people were so willing to help out  I do recommend this game, I've had more fun playing it for the last 2 days than any other game trying to offer an experience similar to this. And I can't stress enough how fun stealth is, honestly I'd buy it again just to experience stealthing Gold & Sharke

Finally at a point where I can genuinely recommend it. I preordered this at launch, and have played a fair bit of the game across it's lifetime. While I was one of the people who felt somewhat let down by the launch, I think Payday 3 was solid as a core game on release, and has only improved across the board in the time since. The content has expanded significantly, and the reworks to the progression system and skill tree system have been stellar. Heist design has also improved a lot since the launch heists, and they've also had fun reworks since launch too. I appreciate that on a lot of levels this game feels the least janky to me of the series. I love Payday 2 and have played it for over a decade, but it's gotten to a point of feeling so janky and dated I only find myself coming back to it for nostalgia or if a friend gets into it for a bit. Payday 3 feels more modern and fluid, and feels a lot better to play combat wise as someone who mostly plays heists on loud. Weapon design also generally feels better, weapons generally have stat blocks that make sense and are at least semi usable across all the difficulties.  The game is still relatively content light (more than Payday 1, less than Payday 2 by a lot) and while there are heists and weapons I wish had been in the game since the start, the game's current state is great and I'm excited to see what further expansion plans they have. New weapons, attachments, customization options, and gang members alongside future heists would be super cool. My only real gripe at this point is that offline heists are still in a bit of a janky state and that leveling weapons feels like it still takes forever, although they do have a pause state in online games now that works when playing solo rounds (not quite the same but at least I can pause playing solo lol)  All in all as a longtime fan, I've generally liked this game across my time playing it, and I think it's only getting better. I'll always let Starbreeze cook, keep up the good work guys

Payday 3 - In my opinion it's a fine production. The launch was not a great beginning, but as I return to the game after I think* about 2 years I see some good progress. Before I even got my hands on it I've spend a lot of hours on Payday 2, which for some time was my most played game.     What I think are the pros and cons of this game:    -Pros-    - The graphics are very good. I like the new style this game offers. Everything in it is more modern, you will notice it during the first heists.    - Reworked armor. I  believe that this makes some difference in the loud heists category. Now the enemies have special plates, that have each their own amount of hp. For players you can customize your armor and swap plates to have an unique type of resistance for a specific damage you are defending from.    - A different approach to heists. It's not all black and white here! What I mean is that they require a bit more smarter approach. I remember lot of heists in payday 2 as "you put the mask on and let's do some robbin". Heists in pd3 are slightly more focused on playing more as a civilian, without putting on mask yet.     - Using guns. In payday 3 guns are pretty fun to use! There are some tweaks that make them a bit different than in payday 2.    - Optimization. The game is nicely optimized, it works smoothly and without issues on my low-mid tier pc.    - Pager rework. You have a certain amount of alarms you can trigger (from 4 to 2 depending on difficulty). But you can get one pager over the limit. If you answer one pager over the limit, the guards will change their standard paths and will start to look for you. During the search time they will instantly arrest you if they spot you in a private area. I think that it is a great addition! It makes sense, that guards start a search instead of pulling the alarm. Just keep in mind that one more pager to that does trigger alarm!    -Cons-    - Game feels too easy. Though you need to play more as a civilian and think about how you can approach each heist, it still feels too simple.   There are 3 types of zones in the game: Public, Private, Secure. In public you are not detected by guards. In private civilians don't detect you, but guards do, and once they do, they will ask you to return to the public zone. Secure, which makes you detectable by all sources.   My biggest issue is the private sone - there are perks that can stun guards and that make your illegal actions legal to the civilians. For example - You broke into the back of a building, it's a private zone, and camera seen you, so a guard is running toward you. He tells you to stop, but you can just stun him with the skill, and just run past him and he simply forgets. It's stupid!  Another one is: You just opened a safe in a private zone, and a civilian comes in. Taking money is an illegal action, but while you have the perk, the civilian ignores it. Or you can lockpick door while not getting detected by civs, cause why not!    - Skills and lack of build creation. In payday 2 you had a lot of ways to make your build for whatever you needed. In payday 3 skills look less creative, it's mostly something like: "There are 3 skill trees in each category (hacker, soldier, etc.) First is stealth focused, other two are just for some offensive stuff. This is simple lack of variety, because it follows this simple scheme every time. First is stealthy, Second throw some tools and a gun perks, Third throw another type of gun, and grenades.     - New camera system. You can access cameras the classic way by getting into the security office, or you can now hack the camera while looking at it and get access to the whole camera network. I would consider it as a good thing, but it has some flaws, that annoy me. For you to access the camera  you have to basically stand in front of it in a very close distance, which makes your detected  In my opinion this should be reworked maybe something like - you don't get access to full network, but you get access to the closest cameras to one you hacked. You should be able to hack it from any distance you want, but the further you'd be your connection would be worse. And maybe something like the latest camera you hacked will be synced to your phone, so you will be able to access it anywhere you want, and that's where the connection mechanic would be important. It would give you the possibility to access camera without standing in front of it like a total moron!    - Removed preplanning. It's gone, you can only buy favors, but I found no menu to actually plan and all.    - Lack of puzzles. There are same puzzles in almost each heist. For example: you have a box of 4 switches and only one is correct. This thing will reappear in every single heist you will think of! Or locked doors that require to find some phone or keycard to access. It's not terrible, but the game needs a more variables in this topic.    -What I couldn't classify as a pro nor con-    - There are no body bags which makes dropping body very annoying due to character ragdoll. It sometimes glitches in the doors, or it falls not where you want it to fall. Body bags were just a lot simple!    - There's now 4 difficulty levels and not 6 compared to the payday 2. I do think that it fits the playstyle, though. The game has a lot less scaling than in was in pd2. What I mean is that you can not make crazy builds that really affect your game, and it cuts a piece of what you can do in the game, because you would simply be unable to withstand the 6th difficulty tier.    - Each player can mark only one target at a time. It's horrible! You can not mark a camera and guard at the same time! You either pick the cam, or the guard. It may be my misusing of the mark option, and if there is a way of marking multiple targets, then it is too hard to find for a ordinary player!!    - Loot bags are not detectable in public areas. It makes some gameplay changes. I wanted to put it here but I don't think that it breaks the gameplay.    My final thoughts are that the Payday 3 is a good game. It still needs a lot of improvements, but I see some future for it, if the team will continue working and they won't shut down the servers. Though it has to be said: You will have more fun in Payday 2, and it's a cheaper alternative. Game needs more heists and puzzles and a bit of reshaping. I like the team, you can feel that they wanted to make something good, but it lacks some of stuff. I do recommend buying, but you have to really think about it! It won't offer as much as you are paying for, but I believe that more content still comes. I had fun, and will continue to have fun thanks to the modding community, which made me feel more joy from the game than I had before!    That is all I could think of. If anyone has gotten to this point I am very grateful, this was my first serious review. Have a great day, and if you consider buying - have fun!

This game had a really rough launch 2 years ago.    After playing 200 hours of PD3, trust me: it has gotten SO much better since launch.    Yes, it still has problems, specifically in terms of performance.  It seems to have lag 'hiccups' where it will freeze for a second and then resume like nothing happened.  Honestly not a huge issue, just something I've noticed.   The game could also use more content in almost everything (more weapons, masks, heists, characters, enemy types, skills/perks, etc.).   That said, I've already gotten 200 hours out of the already existing content, and it is stellar. Here are just a few things I like about PD3:  Whether you're just reloading a gun or shooting a cop in the face, every animation in the game is exquisite.  Almost every item the player interacts with (i.e. loot bags, buttons, levers, etc.) have a unique animation.  They released an update recently that improved animations for some of the weapons, and now all of the weapons are satisfying to use.  The weapon sounds are overall decent, with the silenced sounds being amazing.  The only weapon I don't particularly like the sound of is the sniper rifle.    Also, while the weapon sounds aren't to my personal liking, the hitsounds are incredibly satisfying.  The gunplay is the selling point to this game, and it does not disappoint.    I'll admit, the skill system could use a bit of love with a few skills needing buffs or reworks.  Overall though, the skills you can use are pretty fun and do make a difference in how you play the game.    Most things in the game are unlocked by playing the game.  Well yeah, no kidding, but what I mean is unlocking a new weapon or weapon mod is unlocked by reaching a certain level rather than just being given to you at random after completing a heist (which is how PD2 did it).    A fair warning: PD3 feels much more like an improved version of PD1 rather than a remaster of PD2.  The game is not nearly as complex or detailed as PD2 in terms of customization.  But if you like the simplicity of PD1, you will absolutely love this game.  Hot take here: I started playing PD2 again just so I could unlock special cosmetic content in PD3. I have not touched PD2 since unlocking the cosmetics in PD3.    In summary, the game is lacking in content, but the content that already exists is amazing.  You should absolutely give PD3 a chance if you haven't.  It's absolutely worth it.

I’ve put a decent amount of time into Payday 3, and honestly… it’s fun, but frustrating at the same time.  When it works, it really works. Pulling off a clean stealth run with your mates or randoms feels amazing. The gunplay feels smoother than Payday 2, the movement is better, and the whole “things slowly going wrong” vibe during a heist still gives that chaos the series is known for. Going loud and barely escaping with bags flying everywhere is still peak Payday.  But it’s hard to ignore the problems.  At launch it was a mess. Servers barely worked, and because it’s online-only, you literally couldn’t play. That killed a lot of the hype instantly. Even now, while it’s more stable, it still feels like there’s not enough content. There aren’t many heists, and after a while you start repeating the same jobs over and over. The progression system feels grindy too — like you’re unlocking things slower than you should.  Solo play is pretty dead. The AI teammates aren’t great, and it’s obvious the game is built around real players. If you’ve got a solid squad, it’s a good time. If you don’t, it’s nowhere near as enjoyable.  It also just feels… smaller than Payday 2. Not necessarily worse mechanically, but lighter on personality and content. Payday 2 had years of updates and chaos behind it, so maybe it’s unfair to compare, but right now Payday 3 doesn’t feel like the big step forward people expected.  Would I recommend it? If you’ve got friends to play with and you liked Payday 2, yeah — you’ll have fun. If you’re expecting a huge evolution or loads of content from day one, you might be disappointed.  It’s not a bad game. It just launched too early and hasn’t fully caught up to its potential yet.

PAYDAY 3 takes the foundational systems from its predecessors and retools them into a modern, fluid, and teamwork-centric heist experience where every phase of a job — from recon to getaway — is mechanically engaging and strategically rich.  🪩 Heist Flow & Objective Systems  Heists in PAYDAY 3 are structured as multi-stage mechanical loops rather than linear missions. You don’t just “get in, grab the cash, and get out.” Instead, each job has:  - Pre-heist recon mechanics — scanning hotspots, choosing entry points, and weighing security measures, - Dynamic objectives — like disabling networks, cracking vaults, or manipulating digital systems, - Branching paths — where action choices change encounter pacing and enemy response in real time.  These aren’t checklist tasks — they’re mechanical decision nodes that alter AI behavior, enemy spawn patterns, and payout risk in measurable ways.  🎯 Combat & Team Mechanics  Combat feels weighty, responsive, and balanced around coordination:  - Weapon feel is intentional — recoil, accuracy, firing cadence, and reload speed all matter mechanically, - Cover isn’t cosmetic — it physically impacts enemy focus and incoming fire density, - Suppression, flanking, and angles actively factor into damage mitigation and success.  Enemy AI reacts dynamically to your team’s pace. They flank, take cover, call reinforcements, and escalate threat levels based on how the crew performs, which turns firefights into short tactical puzzles rather than scripted waves.  🔄 Stealth vs Loud Loop — Real Mechanical Choices  Like its predecessors, PAYDAY 3’s stealth isn’t just a “stealth mode.” It’s a full tactical subsystem with its own rules:  - Sound propagation and detection range are tracked mechanically, - Cameras, guard patterns, and alert thresholds all feed into a shared detection system, - One mistake can shift the entire mission into loud, which alters enemy spawn rates, reinforcement pacing, and objective timing.  This isn’t surface-level — it affects pacing, resource use, and opportunity windows. Stealth feels like a different style of gameplay, not just quieter shooting.  👥 Skills, Roles & Crew Synergy  PAYDAY 3 embraces class-agnostic synergy rather than rigid roles. You customize your loadout, perks, gadgets, and abilities to craft functionally distinct playstyles:  - Support tools like ECM jammers and drones affect enemy mechanics and timing loops, - Healing and armor buffs alter how your team sustains through prolonged engagements, - Gadget interplay (trip mines, flash grenades, smoke screens) becomes a mechanical toolkit for controlling crowds or delaying enemy advances.  Teamwork is mechanically reinforced: coordinated ability use creates emergent effects that feel greater than the sum of parts, not just additive damage.  🛠️ Loadouts & Tools as Tactical Systems  Gear isn’t just numbers — it’s mechanical utility:  - Weapon mods change handling, recoil, and concealment — which directly feeds into stealth viability and combat efficiency, - Tools affect pacing (drills, hacking minigames, distractions), - Consumables (first-aid kits, ammo packs, deployables) all activate in ways that influence encounter success, not just stats.  Every item has a role — and choosing when to use it is part of the tactical loop.  📈 Risk & Reward Amplified  Difficulty scaling in PAYDAY 3 isn’t just HP and damage sponges. It’s mechanical escalation:  - Enemy AI modifier tiers, - Faster reinforcement timers, - Stricter stealth detection windows, - More complex objectives  Higher risk equals higher payout mechanically — better loot, better XP, and more unlocks. These choices are strategic, not cosmetic.  10/10 — tight combat, deep tactical systems, and heist mechanics that reward coordination, strategy, and smart execution.

I was unsure if I should buy this game considering all the negative comments around it, and to be honest they're not completely wrong but they're also blown out of proportion, sure, the game has quite a bit of missing content compared to its predecessor, but I don't think anyone was expecting a payday 2 level experience when this game launched. As of this review, I can confidently say this game is worth a shot and is quite a fun experience.  There is one big drawback however. The monetisation. I don't feel like 30€ for a game is expensive, but it is expensive in the context that you also need to pay an extra 5-10€ for each update to unlock all the content. I perosnally bought it on a sale, and I would advise you do the same.  As for the gameplay itself, it differs quite a bit to Payday 2, the stealth parts feel more immersive, more interesting, and at the same time more tedious. The addition of the Lead Guard mechanic in higher difficulties should pose a challenge, but in reality it is nothing more than a stupid adition considering if you get cornered by him the stealth run is basically over since his pager will call every minute, without a stop. That wouldn't be so bad if you would have, uh, 4 pagers, which you don't since the number of pagers gets lowered to 2 in Overkill difficulty. That itself is not a bad change, makes it feel more realistic, but the Lead Guard mechanic just screws it up, they could've probably implemented it in a better way, or not, I'm not a game developer.  Loud feels a bit more intense, doesn't feel harder, feels easier than before, but it does feel a lot more like a proper heist game, the changes to the enemies, the way the enemies act, the music kicking in, honestly a really good experience. I would say that it is a worthy successor to Payday 2, even if right now it's not in the best state.  It's like a bottle of wine, it will only get better with time.

I have been playing Payday since the original Payday: the Heist, and it has to be said, while I do suggest this game for those who enjoy the heist subgenre, this unfortunately comes with some caveats. Payday 3 is an evolution of the series, but not in an entirely positive way. The game's core systems are effective, but pared back in a way that makes objectives feel a lot more uniform than in payday 2, which limits heist replayability, something that is very important for a game that relies on that very replayability as a core element of it's gameplay loop. With that said, I find both the stealth and shooting mechanics to be much more reliable, which wins it some solid points. The new upgrade system likewise has been simplified (no more weapon part packages for one), making it much easier to build your weapons as you'd like without the randomisation factor, but that simplicity is let down somewhat by how opaque things are. It's great that I'm not jumping through hoops, but that doesn't help me if i don't have a solid idea of what the green and red bars actually mean practically in a heist.    In short, the game has been streamlined and modernized. The problem is, that has come at the cost of, well, long-term game-health. Personally, I still enjoy the game enough to regularly play it, particularly since the devs do seem determined to fix the issues and get back on steady footing; but it is hard to ignore that Payday 2 is just a better game at the moment, multiple years on. Skills 2.0 went a long way, and I look forward to seeing how things develop, but given how few people are playing right now, you will likely need some friends to play with if you want to get started.    So;    Pros:   Improved core gunplay  Reduced screen clutter  Good aesthetic sense  Better onboarding and explanation of systems    Cons:  'Samey' objectives  Less build diversity  Fewer (and Shorter) heists    Whether that makes it worthwhile to play for you is going to depend on what parts of the payday series interest you. For me, it is still a positive experience, but one that still needs work to catch up to its predecessor.

this game so good when you don't got someone in your ear telling you it's terrible for not being payday 2  nah but on a real note, I haven't touched this game since buying it on launch, where I felt it was a bit boring and lacking in content, especially compared to its longstanding predecessor. but, with all the updates that have come out over the past two years, it finally feels like a game worth playing and exploring. the new skill system, while still in its early, kinda rough stages, is leagues more interesting than the first iteration, offering interesting tradeoffs in builds whilst also giving weapon types their own roles and exclusive perks.  alongside this is the multitude of other systematic changes, like the new armor system, which allows for deep customization of your defenses for various playstyles, and the unique skills for each overkill weapon (killstreak weapons for the uninitiated), among other things. all of these things combine to make buildcrafting have the same depth, if not more so, than payday 2, which was one of my favorite parts of that game. or any game, for that matter.  there are still some issues with the game, it's nowhere near perfect. like, the progression past max level is still rather lackluster, and a true offline/p2p mode would be really nice to have. but there's still absolutely something enjoyable here. this version of payday still has the much more interesting stealth mechanics and hostage mechanics, which were the things I enjoyed most about payday 3 at launch. but now, there's more fun tools to play with, alongside a solid core gameplay loop of universal mechanics  overall, if you're looking for a fresh, slightly more grounded take on a heisting game compared to payday 2, payday 3 is definitely worth giving a try

Is a fun enough time to roll around in. Better if you have some friends, still works well without. Gunplay's solid and there's enough variety in guns and skill loadouts to play differently. Biggest weakness is the heist selection doesn't feel super strong. Hard to pin down why. With Payday 2's colossal heist pedigree its hard to be objective given how long that game had to mature. I'd pick up Payday 2 one way or another, if you play that game to death like I have Payday 3 may have some legs. As a standalone game, 3 is pretty good. It's just damn hard to live up to 2, but its getting there.  For one thing, the new skills update is a *massive* boon for the game. The former skill system was deeply flawed with well-intentioned choices to make the fluid line-system work with using the Edge, Grit, and Rush mechanic as a way to try to glue it together. While I think in a different world it could've worked well, having things like "Shoot 35 bullets" and "Swap your weapons" be methods to gain Edge, it had a deep micromanagement problem. This just wasn't fun to deal with and didn't achieve the goals they were going for with trying to push for certain playstyles enabling skills.  Now, it's the tried and true: Skill trees with a focus per branch and increasing point cost. No more dealing with getting buffs through odd actions, things either just work or work off of way more straightforward triggers. Much cleaner and simpler to setup a build and overall covering more gameplay options than before. The differing skill point costs also fixed most loadouts being a muddy mix of core overachieving skills plus some random junk thrown in there. Skill setups are much more focused now which defines playstyles a whole lot better.  Cobbling together loadouts was what attracted me to come back for a bit. Just wanted to throw out that I greatly appreciate this skill overhaul because *mother of god* did I hate the old system. Again, not a bad core concept, but what we've got now is so much better than before.

Even though it's a skeleton in comparison to PD2, those bones are much more refined. Far less jank, far less content; better gameplay, less variety. It's a mixed bag. My favorite parts are the armor rework and how shields can be countered with any weapon.     I'd say it's worth the $30 and deserves more players than it has, but it needs a faster development cycle if it wants to stay relevant. It took them 2 years for the game to be a good recommendation.

Once again wishing Steam would let me add a "neutral" review but I'll put it as positive with a huuuuuuuuuge asterisk for now   To keep it brief; I haven't played much of the other Payday games but the only real reason I enjoyed this game was because I had a friend to play it with 90% of the time. The gameplay gets stale pretty damn quick especially for Stealth - which ended up being my preferred way to play. There's fun to be had for sure, 90 hours is a great amount to get but I feel like this game coulda been fun for a lot longer.  To extend my thoughts:  I think my biggest complaint is how the guns just feels bad. The attachment system is incredibly limited and doesn't really explain anything at all. The guns themselves don't feel particularly great to shoot and it's one of the biggest things I wish they'd address. I haven't tested the new skill tree rework and I don't really intend to - it's not enough to warrant me redownloading the game.  My last big complaint is how most of the latest updates are locked behind DLC's. Some are free thankfully but so many are paid, I believe you'd need to pay about triple the base price to get all the DLC's? The weapon selection with no DLC's is quite sad and while the base game is only £25, I still feel like a lot of the work being put into the game is what we should've expected upon release - they're having to make amends for a bad release but are tacking on a price tag for things.   I don't hate the game and I'm glad they didn't just ditch it, but it's really not good practice to release a half-baked game and slowly "fix" it over the next 2+ years. I'd say it's worth picking up, especially on sale.

Despite the bad start, Payday 3 has only been getting better and better. At this point, the game is genuinely a very fun time, builds are getting crazier, dodge is back, and the shooting just feels good. If you were on the fence before because of how it began, I can easily recommend getting it now.

I think the game is at a point where I would start recommending it to people, especially with the recent "Skills 2.0" update. And for context, I played the game when it first launched; bugs, issues and all.    The game has come a long way from it's launch with the addition of a couple things that I saw that I thought were notable. First was the ability to play in a "Solo" game. You no longer need to wait to connect to server, which massively improved load time. And when you have to restart a mission for any reason, there's no massive wait time like there was before. Which is a big plus for me.    I understand people are still upset about the fact that you need to be online in order to play the game at all, but that's not really a deal breaker for me.    Now with "Skills 2.0" update, the game feels a lot better. We're back to the old Payday 2 skill tree system, and I am here for it. Old builds from Payday 2 are now possible in the game. Not to the same extent as they were in Payday 2, but they are present. There's no longer a need to unlock individual skill sets, which makes customization a lot smoother and take less time.       There are some things I think the game still needs to improve on, but I think so far that everything is moving in the right direction and is being set up for these changes.    Firstly, the loadout system needs reworked. I would love there to be a way I could go without armor entirely, that way I could get the most out of the certain skills. Additionally, it can be a bit confusing trying to find the weapons I want, so something I would like to see is some weapon organization or filter implemented.     Secondly, the new skill system still needs some tweaks and work done to it before it can be called perfect, but it is a colossal step in the right direction. Certain skills need altered or buffed, while other skills can be reworked as other changes are implemented.     But at the end of the day, I think the game is ready to come back into the light. It's now where it should be by no means, but it's making the necessary corrections to get there and I'm happy for it. I don't want to see this game die, and I want nothing more than to see it make a return No Man's Sky style. I think that would be spectacular.

I remember playing PAYDAY 2 the first time on the PS3 as a kid. It was a lacking game. I didn't know that back in the day until I had all of the fun there was to have in it, and it took a while to reach that cap.  Fast forward to the CRIMEWAVE EDITION on the PS4, the game no longer felt lacking. It had an awesome amount of content. This was only 2-3 years later. And, in today's measure, I still didn't know it was a lacking game for how richer it got until I'd pick it up on Steam.  PAYDAY 2 was never a linear game initially, but this was just fine because of how the game made me feel like I was getting near unending new heist opportunities. This was very immersive.  In the later years, I thought to pick up PAYDAY 2 and watch it expand into the ridiculously rich game it is. It now has a career mode that shows proper continuity in progression and story for casual players. Because of how ridiculously rich the game got, it got a bit more difficult to navigate how to complete the game fully, so it added a linear element to the game that is a very welcome addition.  I never bothered to understand the world PAYDAY 2 had build, but I didn't need to bother. PAYDAY 2 always made that part easy, before and after the career mode - from the start to the end of its development cycle. Its story always had something brewing in the background, and it always made ridiculously awesome cinematic promotional content. The game was fun, but it was much more than a game - and this always made me want to play the game more.  PAYDAY 2's story is a story that would sometimes change from very serious tones to very comical tones - and it's story would change its course every now and then to help implement collaborative content from other titles like HOTLINE MIAMI and John Wick. Even more ridiculously, Goat Simulator, but it does it in such good way that I never thought it wasn't welcome in the game.  When PAYDAY 3 released, I thought it was following the same trajectory, so I kept a positive perspective.  PAYDAY 3 is a very fun game to play. It seems like it has the whole world going for itself, but it forgot that part somewhere along the way. It is a lacking game, and - unlike my experience from PAYDAY 2 - it shows from the start. The game shows all of the available heists in a way that's presented almost linearly but gives no story. It's sometimes too cleanly presented that it's devoid of a specific criminal charm PAYDAY 2 had. The game does little to make me feel like there's a world behind PAYDAY 3, and it doesn't have much promotional or in-game content that tells me this in an easy way. I think it's very clear that PAYDAY 3 is desperately trying to gather engagement in its collaboration with PUBG - which I don't think complements the game whatsoever.  It's so unfortunate that it's just a game. A very lacking generically presented game that betrays the potential it has. PAYDAY 3 needs a lot more than just more game content to feel more than a game.

Будучи ветераном второй части, да и немало наиграв в первую, я с большим искушением ждал триквел. И пусть у Starbreeze дела были плохи, а вторую часть воскресили в основном для того, чтобы заиметь хоть какие-то деньги на разработку следующей части, что есть плохой знак, я всё равно не бросил надежд и посмел ожидать чуда. Его, как вы понимаете, не случилось. Трейлеры выглядели многообещающе. Несмотря на отвратительные движок и графику, игра демонстрировала интересные мелкие нововведения, которых не было во второй части, и которым вполне было под силу разбавить тамошний геймплей. Как оказалось, этого недостаточно, чтобы собрать игру, да ещё и такую, чтобы превзошла предыдущую часть.  Увидев множество отрицательных отзывов, связанных, в основном, с бедностью контента, я решил дать игре время реабилитироваться. И вот, прошло почти три года, а игра получила скидку в -50% - пора, решил я. Купив игру я не поверил своим глазам. Она выглядела как какая-то мобильная поделка, вот честно. И дело даже не в графике, а в очень неприятном стилистическом исполнении с большими пунктами в меню и странными анимациями гг.  Ну окей, подумал я, запустил ограбление и... вот честно, такого разочарования я не испытывал давно. Вроде бы и играется бодро, вроде бы есть что-то прикольное, но это всё выглядит как очередная обнова ко второй части. Ничего сверхуникального нет, а нововведения, вроде стелса без маски или очередные гаджеты - не пришей кобыле хвост.   Тем не менее, потенциал у игры всё ещё чувствуется, но реализован он крайне фрагментарно. Вместо цельного развития серии - набор разрозненных идей, часть из которых работает, а другая часть выглядит лишней. На фоне того, как PAYDAY 2 развивалась годами и обрастала контентом, текущая динамика PAYDAY 3 вызывает скорее опасения, чем надежду. В текущем состоянии я не могу порекомендовать игру даже фанатам серии - разумнее обратить внимание на вторую часть, которая по-прежнему ощущается более цельной и насыщенной.

Payday 3 has mixed reviews for a reason. Yes, the game is better than launch. Yes, the game has some reworked mechanics that make it a bit more enjoyable than doing the same actions in Paydays 2, such as lock picking and item interactions in general. Yes, stealth is so much better now. But I think there is 2 things that this game lacks that made Payday 2 great: Personality and Perk Decks.  The personality is very important to Payday's identity and is the reason why a lot of fans didn't really care for the crossovers and Joy being added to Payday 2. Payday has always had this apathetic and snarky middle finger attitude to the capitalistic system and authority, the gang is literally a bunch of psychos that want the world's riches to themselves, but Payday 3 has this very limp adaptation of that vibe, even beating Payday 2 at it's worst when it would drop the ball on maintaining it. It feels more like a game that was inspired by Payday but with a better budget than a proper sequel.  This also bleeds a little into Perk Decks not being in the game. I never knew how much Perk Decks filled out the identity of the gameplay until it was removed and replaced with a fairly lackluster skill tree. I get why it was done, figuring out what Perk Deck works with what skills and what loadout isn't easy but once you found what worked for you and your crew, it created this feeling of being a proper member of the gang, playing your role and insuring that the heist will be pulled off. Dumbing down the gameplay to appeal to more gamers kinda hurt what made Payday what it is. It makes it feel more generic and stale, like I'm gaining money for cosmetic nonsense instead of to improve my build.   Even if this game did do more to make it more like Payday 2, I don't think it can live up. This is even more apparent when you realize that they are still making content for Payday 2 and their latest update as of me writing this is the PUBG collab which no one asked for, no one has said anything positive about it, and I have no clue how collabing with an irrelevant game is gonna do any good for Payday as a brand.   The game is good but it could have been great, it deserves it's reputation.

Review: A promising game covered in diarrhea. (I played the game by “sailing the seas” method for 3 years. I finally bought this just to write this review, and I bought it at full price out of pity, not because it earned it.)  Good Side: =========  This game genuinely feels like an upgrade from my beloved Payday 2. The graphics, guns, animations, and sounds all just click, and I love that part of it. Base heists are somewhat fun, and the AI pathfinding and difficulty scaling have improved a lot compared to the last game. The stealth system feels more readable and less janky in some situations, and when it works, it actually feels smoother than PD2. I also like the vendor system, it gives a proper underworld black market vibe. Skills are fairly balanced and not completely broken like some PD2 builds used to be. Gunplay overall feels heavier and more grounded, and the hit feedback is genuinely satisfying when the game isn’t busy tripping over itself.  Bad Side: ========  Oh boy, there’s a lot of shjt to unpack here. First and biggest issue is the complete lack of offline mode. Payday has always been a co-op shooter, but requiring a live server was never a necessity. Starbreeze forced this online-only crap down everyone’s throat. People begged for offline mode, got promises, waited years, and then got hit with “not feasible.” That’s not just a middle finger to your core player base, it’s a self-inflicted death sentence for preservation and long-term playability.  Offline Mode Rant:  Dear Starbreeze, we know exactly why you did this. Your fanbase is not stupid or naive. This entirely contrived online-only BS exists so you can gatekeep progression, check DLC ownership, and keep your little store ecosystem running. All to prevent people from saving a few dollars in the most ineffective way possible.  But let’s be honest, did it work? No. Your servers are barely more secure than a school computer lab. People have been playing this game and its DLCs for free anyway (including myself). So what exactly did you achieve? you shat on your major playerbase who wanted offline mode and people can pirate and cheat in this game without a worry about anything. Yee-fcking-haw for you.  -Rant Over-  Back to the rest of the game. The UI is a disaster. Skills and favors are poorly explained, not color-coded, and often vague to the point of being useless. Nothing is intuitive. The vendor menu feels like it was designed without anyone asking “how would a player actually use this?” Everything is scattered, inconsistent, and clunky.  The story is another issue. It contradicts itself and feels like it actively disrespects what Payday 2 built up. Instead of evolving the narrative properly, it feels like a reset that ignores its own legacy. It has none of the charm, buildup, or identity that made PD2’s world memorable.  The art direction is inconsistent at best and ugly at worst. Character portraits look AI-generated half the time. Voice acting feels flat, lifeless, and rushed. Shade is especially painful. She is supposed to be a central mastermind figure like Bain or Locke, but instead she sounds like she’s reading lines off a script she doesn’t care about. No presence, no personality, nothing. I genuinely wish there was a mute option for certain lines.  Movement has potential but is constantly fighting itself. It feels delayed, stiff, and inconsistent, especially when compared to the tight, responsive movement of PD2. Everything feels like it has input lag baked into it.  And optimization, or whatever you want to call it, is another mess. Yes, it’s UE4, but that’s not an excuse. Plenty of UE4 games run significantly better. The performance feels unpolished, unstable, and inconsistent even on hardware that should easily handle it.  There’s also a lack of meaningful content depth. After the initial novelty wears off, heists start blending together, progression doesn’t feel rewarding, and replayability suffers hard compared to Payday 2’s long-term loop. It feels like a game designed around systems instead of fun.  I don’t really have the energy to keep going. I’ve been playing this series for almost 10 years and I love it to death. But it feels like the franchise doesn't love me back. Payday 2 had a blueprint for greatness, and instead of refining it, they ended up being "Generic" in the greedy pursuit of Uniqueness.

Although the game has improved since launch, some of the design choices are “interesting” or downright bad. The devs seemingly don’t playtest anything, or if they do, it’s on the lowest difficulty. The balance is very hit or miss, especially when doing loud solo heists, since the bots seemingly have the mental capacity of a 3-year-old, the speed of a sloth, the survival instincts of a panda, and can’t hit the broad side of a barn. They will almost always just stand out in the open waiting to be turned into Swiss cheese by the SWAT team, stay in a nader’s gas cloud, or get stunlocked by techie drones. Shouting at them does nothing, so it forces you to go out into the open to revive them or let them die since you can’t disable them to begin with. It’s like looking after three kids in a swimming pool who can’t swim and are constantly drowning. Honestly, if you can play with friends or try your luck at the roulette wheel with randoms, you will have a lot more fun than playing solo with bots.    There’s a variety of heists, but with a lot less spectacle and grandeur than PD2, which is fine — I get that the devs wanted to make it more grounded this time. My main issue is that most of them kind of feel the same since there’s very little variety in terms of objectives. Stealth is okay, I guess; it’s not particularly hard. The difficulties might as well be renamed to “piss easy,” “very easy,” “easy,” and “hard.” I can’t really tell any difference except when going from “very hard” to “overkill,” which has an exponential jump in difficulty compared to the other difficulties — and even then, it’s not that hard.    It is also very clear when looking at major updates like “Armor 2.0” that the devs don’t really want to listen to feedback from the community. What do I mean, I hear you ask? Well, the biggest complaint about the launch armor system was that it didn’t allow for much build variety. Nothing really mattered other than armor bags since health disappeared in two seconds, medic bags were useless, and ammo wasn’t really an issue with most weapons, so ammo bags weren’t picked very often. It basically boiled down to everybody running armor bags and building around that. Most of the community didn’t like that and preferred the armor system in PD2, so they chose to do a sort of hybrid system to add more build variety, which on paper sounds good, but in my opinion the execution is very poor.    The three different armor types are poorly balanced and still push you into very limited builds, since the only viable plates are the “Resistant Plate” — with no penalty to downs, 40% damage resistance, and the fastest regen time of 1.2s (although it doesn’t fully regen) — and the “Adaptive Plate” — with −1 down per plate, 25% damage resistance, and a regen time of 1.8s that fully regenerates if it doesn’t break, which is still a solid choice. The “Impact Plate” gives −1 down per plate, 0% damage resistance, and a 3s regen time, but it fully regenerates even when broken. So not only do you lose a down per plate, but it disappears as fast as health and has a god-awful regen time. Its only use is for running one plate, since the way combining multiple plates works with regen time is (regen time × plates = total armor regen time). This means if you are running, for example, four Impact Plates, your armor won’t regen for 12 seconds while not getting shot. This makes it basically useless, so in the end it still pushes you into armor bags being the most important.    The new skill changes are another step in the right direction, but this game is still a long way from having the build and heist variety that PD2 had. This decreases the amount of experimentation, which is what I enjoyed the most in PD2.

This review was wrote before the news drop, the changes are stuff like enemy waves and overkill weapons stuff like that  Look you can wait and you can wait for promises of the game getting better but we have been doing this for almost 3 years, the point is, they wanted to make a live service game but make it not live service, the lack of communication from the dev's makes this insanely hard too, we go into "We are so back" phases every time a DLC drops, and then the games doom and gloom for the next 2 months, Payday 3 Isn't worth your time after almost 400 hours of this game I genuinely cannot recommend it, it depresses me that this is a payday game.  The gameplay is just fine nothing else, if its comparable to any other first person shooter, it has a slide it has a jump and you can shoot that's basically it, the heisting mechanics aren't that great either, feels like all you do in stealth is wait, this is the one time I actually prefer payday 2s stealth because it felt like I was using my skills to move fast and dodge sightlines, payday 3 barely has any of this, most of the time you are just going to be waiting for circles or loading bars, when it gets really bad is definitely touch the sky's stealth, just felt boring and unnecessary.  The music is just okay, I really don't want to comment on the music since its just okay, there's not insane great tracks like in payday 2 all of them are just okay.  The customization for weapons and masks are really good that's one of the things I do like about this game, its pretty in depth for what you can do but I do wish you could maybe like pick what parts of your gun you can paint like one at a time, instead of having segments same with masks too, would be super cool to see some kind of "make your own mask" type thing but I feel as if people could use that to just be offensive too.  The enemies aren't actually that good, I play on overkill and I feel as if enemies aren't as smart as they are in payday 2, it feels like we always get 1 techie and 1 dozer and a few "Zappers" (Just Tasers from payday 2 without the gun and only the taser.) and then the rest of the enemies are just generic shotgun enemies or automatic rifle enemies, not much variation like in payday 2s enemy waves, like yes the enemies do get stronger with most waves near the ending of a heist, but it doesn't feel like they get harder to kill now they just have more armor that gets bypassed with obliterated by headshots. what I would do to change this is maybe adding something that allows during waves, smaller waves of enemies, like maybe instead of 1 dozer there can be 2 or 3, and once those are beat it goes into the next smaller wave, I do think we need a lot more enemies like in payday 2, it felt like I was always seeing different types of dozers or enemies in different waves.  If this game had maybe monthly drops like separate updates for like the bug fixes and changes, and maybe a DLC or a heist every month I would recommend this game, but as it stands, this game has and is in a rough shape, I do think they are trying their best to make this game as good as they can because a lot of people want a lot of different things, but their different things isn't a good enough take, I wouldn't say this is good nor bad objectively, I can see some fun out of it, but not a lot, once you get to level 150, there's really no reason to keep playing the game, I am happy that they are fixing a lot of the bad decisions from launch but, launch was almost 3 years ago at this point, so this is one of the times I do think steam needs a neutral rating.  TL;DR After 400 hours of payday 3, the game is shallow with good ideas poorly executed, and weak stealth with inconsistent support. "Not worth it right now"

Had high hopes to the successor to Payday 2. Pre-ordered and played early access. After the game released it was unplayable/servers down. Then a month later, the player base was nearly gone. The stealth mechanics are a step up from 2 but thats the only upside in my opinion. Gun play feels stale, the heists lack identity, and the characters dont feel as charismatic as they did in the previous release. Get it on a cheap sale but at full price, with 7.99 dlc heists, i cannot justify that. Maybe in a few years this game will be more well rounded but as of the start of 2026, i have no desire to reinstall again.

The heists are boring and all follow a similar formula of doing the same type of objectives in different locations. The gunplay can be fun, it's certainly a step up from payday 2 (imo) but that doesn't really mean much if you're doing the same thing over and over again.  Speaking of which, the cops feel so bland in comparison to payday 2. I find it genuinley hard to tell certain specials apart given the same drab color they all wear. Yeah, they're apart of a force sure, sure whatever, But when you see a bulldozer, i expect to recognize it upon first sight, not aimlessly wander my eyes wondering why my armor suddenly was torn in half among the ocean of basic cops of which the bulldozer looks exactly alike. The bulldozer charge is cool, though, ill say that much.  But otherwise the game is a massive dissapointment and probably one of the most hilarious instances of a company ruining one of the easiest "we've got this in the bag" moments they could have ever had.  As of writing this there are only 500+ plus people playing this while payday still has about 20,000+ What does that tell you?  Truth is, the game was rigged from the start.
